coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of white light emitting carbon quantum dots: Fabrication of white fluorescent film and optical sensor applications
coredata.dc:creator: Jagannathan, Mohanraj
Dhinasekaran, Durgalakshmi
Soundharraj, Prabha
Rajendran, Saravanan
Vo, Dai-Viet N.
Prakasarao, Aruna
Ganesan, Singaravelu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have reported on the facile synthesis of white light-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQD) from corncob by hydrothermal method. This CQD has a broad emission from 380 nm to 650 nm with high photoluminescence intensity even after three months of shelf-life and stable at variable pH conditions. The presence of Si and N impurities in the biomass gives a greater advantage in producing white light emission with high quantum yield (54%) and enhanced lifetime at ambient conditions. The CQD is highly sensitive towards DNA, paracetamol, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ fluorescence sensing and signifies its application as a multi-modal fluorescence sensor. The results of optical sensitivity calculated from the linear range of 1‐10 ng/mL, 0.10‐0.30 mg/mL, 2.5446 ng/mL, 0.0694 mg/mL, 0.3103–1.5515 μM/mL, 0.4299–4.7293 μM/mL, 1.3010 μM/mL and 0.05–2.5 μM/mL. The limit of detection is 2.5446 ng/mL, 0.0694 mg/mL, 0.8641 μM/mL, 1.2454 μM/mL, 1.3010 μM/m, 0.8550 μM/mL and 2.8562 μM/mL, respectively. And also, the relative standard deviation values of 2.30%, 4.46%, 1.79%, 1.84%, 0.26%, 1.23% and 0.35% are evidences its possibility of development towards potential optical sensor applications. Flexible white light-emitting sheets were fabricated from the CQD, illuminates uniform brightness, and has good color reproducibility and higher stability under various UV light excitation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The sonochemical method promoted hydrothermal preparation of carbon quantum dots from the carbonized biomass of corn cob (e.g. in order to obtain an intercalated graphitic oxide, carbonized biomass has been sonicated in the presence of hydrogen peroxide followed by the dropwise added ammonia solution to further exfoliate it into sheets then heat treatment were applied. To the dried powder, double distilled water was added and poured into the Teflon autoclave for hydrothermal treatment. After this, the solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper and the resultant will be the golden yellow colloidal solution of CQD), (b) mechanism of CQD synthesis from intercalation, exfoliation, and hydrothermal treatment process. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) SEM images of the biomass-derived biomass depict stacked flakes like structure, an expected morphology of layer graphene structure, (b–e) elemental mapping of graphene shows uniform distribution of carbon (C) random distribution of N, O, and Si, and (f) overall mapping images. (g) TEM images have shown the aggregated spherical CQD with d-spacing in the lattice structure, (inset: SAED pattern depicts the partial crystalline structure, with the bright random spots indicating the possibility of crystalline silica presence), (h, i) magnified view of TEM image and (j) particle size analysis with cumulative spectra. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Raman spectra (Laser-488 nm) shows the presence of defect band (D-band) and G-band (sp2 hybridized lattice structure), the characteristic signature of graphene structure, (b) X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) indicates broad X-ray diffraction, confirms the formation of partially amorphous graphene with silica (*) structure, (c) functional group confirmation using FT-IR spectra and (d) the stability of the CQD is validated from Zeta potential.

(a) UV–Visible absorption spectra maximum of CQD at 265 nm and 350 nm (inset: Tauc plot from UV–Vis spectra), (b) the 2D excitation-emission matrix of CQD, (c) 3D excitation-emission matrix with a surface plot from distinct photoluminescence spectra of CQD, (d) PL emission of CQD at excitation of 350 nm at variable pH (1–14) condition, (e) stability of white emission of CQD against distinct days (inset: pictorial representation for λex=350 nm on distinct days), and (f) 1931 CIE diagram with co-ordination number for overall spectra (X = 0.18; Y=0.21) and gaussian fitted data (X = 0.39; Y=0.36) (inset: white emission under λex=350 nm).

(a) Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) spectra with residual spectra, and (b) deconvoluted photoluminescence (PL) spectra, (c) bandgap alignment, and (d) effect of quenching on selective detection of various organic molecules, 2+ and 3+ oxidative state metal ions.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the CQD in the presence of various organic molecules and oxidative state metal ions.

(a) Preparation of carbon quantum dots film (e.g. PVA was added into the CQD solution that has been stirred until it becoming a transparent solution and poured onto OHP template followed by drying in order to obtain transparent, foldable CQD sheet. (b) As prepared sheet CQD sheet in the dark condition, (c) CQD sheet under the illumination of Xenon lamp with the excitation of 350 nm emits bright white light, and (d) CQD sheet under UV illumination with the excitation of 395 nm, emits deep white light.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of white light emitting carbon quantum dots: Fabrication of white fluorescent film and optical sensor applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have reported on the facile synthesis of white light-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQD) from corncob by hydrothermal method. This CQD has a broad emission from 380 nm to 650 nm with high photoluminescence intensity even after three months of shelf-life and stable at variable pH conditions. The presence of Si and N impurities in the biomass gives a greater advantage in producing white light emission with high quantum yield (54%) and enhanced lifetime at ambient conditions. The CQD is highly sensitive towards DNA, paracetamol, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ fluorescence sensing and signifies its application as a multi-modal fluorescence sensor. The results of optical sensitivity calculated from the linear range of 1‐10 ng/mL, 0.10‐0.30 mg/mL, 2.5446 ng/mL, 0.0694 mg/mL, 0.3103–1.5515 μM/mL, 0.4299–4.7293 μM/mL, 1.3010 μM/mL and 0.05–2.5 μM/mL. The limit of detection is 2.5446 ng/mL, 0.0694 mg/mL, 0.8641 μM/mL, 1.2454 μM/mL, 1.3010 μM/m, 0.8550 μM/mL and 2.8562 μM/mL, respectively. And also, the relative standard deviation values of 2.30%, 4.46%, 1.79%, 1.84%, 0.26%, 1.23% and 0.35% are evidences its possibility of development towards potential optical sensor applications. Flexible white light-emitting sheets were fabricated from the CQD, illuminates uniform brightness, and has good color reproducibility and higher stability under various UV light excitation.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon and its derivatives are actively involving in various fields owing to their exceptional electronic, chemical, mechanical, and thermal properties (Mohanraj et al., 2020a; Lim et al., 2015). In the family of carbon derivatives, carbon quantum dots (CQD) has unique optical (e.g. excitation depended on emission, high quantum yield, etc.) and chemical properties (Zheng et al., 2015). In 2004, a new class of CQD has been first discovered during the purification of single wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) (Bottini et al., 2006). In 2006, Sun et al. coined the name as CQD and also proved the fluorescence property of this material (Sun et al., 2006). Comparing to the widely used conventional organic fluorophores, and semiconductor-based quantum dots, the fluorescent CQD has low toxicity, excellent optical tunability and photo-stability, and outstanding chemical stability (Prabha et al., 2020b). It paves a way for the applications towards cost-effective electronics, catalysis, energy conversion and storage devices, biosensors, bio-imaging, and a drug delivery system (Mohanraj et al., 2020b; Durgalakshmi et al., 2020). Synthesis of CQD can be achieved both by top-down and bottom-up methods. Microwave heating, pyrolysis, biosynthesis, hydro, and solvothermal, are some of the bottom-up methods; and electrochemical exfoliation, ultrasonic treatment, chemical oxidation, and laser ablation are top-down methods (Anthony et al., 2020; Geng et al., 2019; Emamian et al., 2020; Saravanan et al., 2020). Out of these methods, the hydrothermal method created great attention for the green synthesis of CQD from biomass such as banana, orange peel, papaya, rice husk (Wang et al., 2017c; Bose et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2020), and others. By tuning the synthesis procedures and parameters, in addition to the above-mentioned biomass, some other biomass of rice husk, sugarcane bagasse, and coconut shell, etc (Thambiraj and Shankaran, 2016; Prabha et al., 2019, 2020a) are also been used to the synthesize organosilicon based carbon derivative nanomaterials towards optical sensors and biomedical applications.

Heteroatom doping in carbon quantum atoms has shown significant enhancement in the fluorescence properties of CQD. N/S/P based functionalization at the edges of the CQD influence the blue fluorescent emission property with higher quantum yield compared to pure CQD (Tang et al., 2019). Si-doped carbon quantum dots showed the blue emission with high quantum yield and longer lifetime for its application extended towards bioimaging and multifunctional sensor (Amjadi et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2018). N, P-doped carbon quantum dots produces green emission, act as a carbendazim sensing by FRET mechanism (Yang et al., 2018). Biomass-derived N-doped carbon quantum dots with blue emission emergent as a nanoprobe for Fe3+ ions detection and applied in bioimaging (Qi et al., 2019).

A lot of researchers were looking for the solution to develop low-cost white light emission material, where a major credential is given to semiconductor quantum dots. Semiconductor quantum dots based white emission is considered as a next-generation light source due to their fast response, high operational lifetime, luminous efficiency, and low power consumption (Mondal et al., 2019). The development of semiconductor quantum dots based white emission device has more research gap because it is often made by the combination of either blue and yellow with phosphorus-based materials (e.g. two complementary systems) or blue, green (Zheng et al., 2020) and red (e.g. three complementary systems with various rare-earth-doped and perovskite materials) (Madhu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). In addition to this, a lot of considerable factors such as chromaticity drifts, degradation of luminescence efficiency, and temperature need to be addressed in utilizing this material. On the other hand, semiconductor-based QD contains heavy metal, causes severe toxicity and this minimizes the practical and industrial applications (He et al., 2018). Notably, CQD from biomass-based nanomaterials can be a better alternative owing to their larger abundance, easy availability, low cost, and environmentally friendly (Zhang et al., 2018). In taking advantage of these, in the present work, we reported a facile, economical, and effective sonochemical assisted hydrothermal method to synthesis CQD from corn cob derived biomass. Furthermore, in the awe of considering heteroatom doping in biomass reported elsewhere (Park et al., 2018) in enhancing fluorescence properties, the impurities in the biomass of corn cob charring (e.g. N, Si, etc.) were retained in the preparation of CQD and it gives a highly efficient white light emission. This opens the possibility of broaden applications like sensors (Liu et al., 2019), bioimaging (Anthony et al., 2020), and other various applicable fields. From the prepared CQD colloidal solution we also explored the possibility of its applications as optical sensors for detecting organic molecules and inorganic ions. World Health Organization (WHO) standardized the tolerable concentration for organic molecules and metal ions. Organic molecules and heavy metal ion detection are receiving significant attention due to their pernicious effect on human health as well as on the ecosystem (Cheraghi et al., 2016). The heavy metal ions are one of the most dangerous pollutants that can affect the healthy individual and cause diseases related to kidney dysfunction, Parkinson’s, Wilson’s, Alzheimer’s diseases, DNA damage, oxidative stress, and apoptotic cell death, etc (Carolin et al., 2017; Shahmiri et al., 2013). Also, the pollutant of organic molecules, pharmaceutical drugs, metal ions that are directly exposed to manufacturing, mining, leather processing, pigment production, leads to a lot of environmental pollution (SenthilKumar et al., 2011; Vardhan et al., 2019; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2017; Yaashikaa et al., 2019). Various methods (e.g. Cyclic Voltammetry, Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS), X-ray Fluorescence (XRF), etc.) are employed to quantify the organic molecules and heavy metal compounds in the effluents (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2016; Tavana et al., 2012), whereas those techniques are comparatively expensive than the optical method of detection (Liu et al., 2019). The detection of these organic molecules and inorganic metal ions by fluorescence detection method has created great attention in recent days due to its efficiency and easy to visualize the sensitivity with the naked eye. In our work, we explore the possibility of utilizing the CQD colloid as an inexpensive optical probe to quantify some organic molecules, pharmaceutical drugs and metal ions with high sensitivity and selectivity. The CQD colloid is also used to fabricate flexible sheet, where the white emission shows the possibility to utilize these sheets to fabricate a quantum LED (QLED) in future. The prime novelty of the present work is to synthesis CQD by sustainable method. As the CQD shows excellent fluorescence white light emitting property, it has been utilized for detection of various organic molecules and heavy metal ion detection. The potentiality of the CQD for the fabrication of white light emitting film were also explore and discussed in detail.

Corn cob derived home-made graphitic biomass is used as a starting material. The chemical of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), ammonia solution, hydrochloric acid, and sodium hydroxide was procured from the Merck, calf thymus DNA (Alfa Aesar), bovine serum albumin, urea, ammonium persulfate, thiourea, paracetamol drug (Cipla, India), potassium chloride, magnesium hydroxide, zinc (II) nitrate, strontium (II) nitrate, barium (II) chloride, mercury (II) chloride, nickel (II) sulfate, zirconium (II) chloride, lead (II) acetate, copper (II) nitrate. Bismuth (III) chloride, boric acid, aluminum (III) chloride, lanthanum (III) nitrate, neodymium (III) nitrate, iron (III) chloride, and chromium (III) oxide of each sample were procured as analytical grade and used without further purification. polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Low molecular weight) from Merck is procured for using as a structuring agent in sheet preparation.

Corn was planted and collected by J. Mohanraj from the fields of Tiruvannamalai, Tamil Nadu, India, and the kernels were removed from the surface. The corn cob was dried under sunlight and utilized further to prepare a carbonized biomass by combustion method. 100 mg of this carbonized biomass was sonicated in 75 mL hydrogen peroxide for about 6 h and to this, 25 mL ammonia is added and stirred for 2 h. In order to obtain an intercalated and exfoliated carbon powder, the solution was heated up to 70–80 °C for 12 h. To this dried powder, added 100 mL of double-distilled water (DDH2O). For hydrothermal synthesis, this solution is transferred into the Teflon autoclave and treated at 220 °C for 12 h and the resultant will be a colloidal solution of carbon quantum dots. The procedure for the aforementioned process is schematically shown in Fig. 1a and b.

The white emission CQD quantum yield was calculated by taking freshly prepared quinone sulfate as a reference that has been dissolved in double-distilled water. Quinine sulfate has excitation and an emission wavelength of 350 nm and 450 nm, respectively using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FluoroMax-2, and Fluorolog, HORIBHA Instruments). At the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence spectra of both the quinine sulfate (QS) and CQD were acquired, normalized and the integrated area of the curve was calculated. An integrated fluorescence intensity vs corresponding optical density was plotted, and the obtained linearly fitted values are used to estimate the quantitative information of quantum yield has been calculated using the Eq. (1) (Bose et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of organic molecules, and metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection of organic molecules and metal ions was exemplified by the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQD. Briefly, 2 mL of prepared CQD solution has been added into the cuvette, subsequently, various concentrations of organic molecules and metal ions were added to the above CQD solutions. This mixture was allowed to react for the 30 s to achieve equilibration and further used to observe the fluorescence emission (λex=350 nm) of each sample. Following this, the efficiency of selective detection of these organic molecules and metal ions were calculated using Eq. (2) ( Gao et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection (LOD), and quantification (LOQ), precision, and repeatability calculation
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching effect of CQD was estimated using the standard deviation rule for qualitative and quantitative analysis of optical sensors. The LOD and LOQ are calculated for all the samples on 1 day, 2 days, and 3 days by slope method as per the Eqs. (3) and (4) (Gao et al., 2019).

To confirm the reliability and stability of this proposed method, the inter-day precision, and stability experiment has been further examined by calculating the relative standard deviation (RSD). This method is applicable to measure the absolute value of the coefficient of variation that can express the precision and repeatability (accuracy) of CQD fluorescence quenching effect calculated by Eq. (5) (Liu et al., 2017).
(5)

X 100 %
Where, σ is the standard deviation, and µ is the mean value, respectively.

The optical absorption spectra are measured with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Jasco-V730). Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM-Laser wavelength-488 nm, France) was used to examine the formation of carbon quantum dots, FT-IR spectroscopy (Jasco FT/IR- 6600, USA) was used to record the absorption spectra in the range from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 to further verify the functional group's presence in the carbon quantum dots. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected using a PANalytical X-ray diffractometer utilizing Cu Kα (λ = 1.54Å) radiation. The obtained carbon quantum dot is coated 20 times on the glass substrate then samples were dried at an interval time of 10 min and used for XRD analysis scanning in the 2θ range from 10° to 80°. The peaks present in the XRD pattern were assigned and confirmed by the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS). The morphology and mapping of the carbonized biomass have been visualized from Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS) (Hitachi-N3400, Singapore). High-resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) (HRTEM, JEOL JEM 2100) analysis was carried out to observe the size of the carbon quantum dot size and lattice structure. The particle size of the CQD has been recorded with Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) from Horiba, Japan. The spectrum of photoluminescence (PL), Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC), and Excitation Emission Matrix (EEM) were recorded using FluoroMax-2, Fluorolog, and Aqualog (Horiba Scientific), respectively. The PL spectra were deconvoluted using fityk software and ImageJ software was used to measure the size of the CQD, further, the 3D surface plot was plotted using Origin 2020 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological studies and elemental mapping of carbonized biomass
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the as-carbonized biomass observed using SEM ( Fig. 2a) depicts stacked flakes like structure with the lateral size of 16.10 µm, 3.68 µm, and 0.103 µm as length, breadth, and thickness, respectively. The elemental mapping was observed by EDS, confirms the presence of C, N, O, and Si distribution (Fig. 2b–e). The mapping ratio of the elements, reveals that there is a uniform elemental distribution of carbon (C is indicated by the red dot) with a random distribution of nitrogen (N is indicated by the green dot), oxygen (O is indicated by the blue dot), and silica (Si is indicated by the white dot). The presence of these elements is also comparable with earlier literature (Zhang et al., 2019). The overall elemental distribution of the elements in as-carbonized biomass is shown in Fig. 2f.

Fig. 2g depicts the High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the CQD with the homogenous near-spherical assembly of particles. The inset of Fig. 2h and i represents the image of a single quantum dot with measured lattice spacing by ImageJ software, where the measured lattice d-spacing is found to be 0.194 nm. The average particle size of CQD is calculated using ImageJ shows a narrow size distribution in the range of ~4 nm. The SAED pattern of the CQD (Inset: Fig. 2g), gives another characteristic evident for the combined amorphous with crystalline structure (Liang et al., 2016), where the diffracted bright spot might be due to the presence of a trace amount of silica bonded with carbon (Akshara et al., 2018), and this is also been compared with the EDS spectrum (Fig. S1). Fig. 2j represents the DLS spectra for colloidal CQD, which measures the hydrodynamic radii of the particles, gives a maximum size distribution from 4 nm to 6 nm, comparable with the TEM results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on XRD, FTIR, and zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed structural characterizations were carried out to understand the formation of CQD ( Fig. 3a–d). Fig. 3a discloses the Raman spectra of the CQD, shows two predominant broad peaks, at ~1370 cm− 1 and ~ 1599 cm−1, corresponds to defects in the basal plane of graphene structure termed as D-band and the sp2 hybridized lattice structure termed as a G-band, respectively. Remarkably, the ID/IG intensity ratio is about 0.9, which is also evidence of a highly crystalline order attained during the preparation of CQD (Ding et al., 2018). This could likely be originating from its larger defect density of the amorphous carbon structure from the as-carbonized biomass. Following this, XRD has been used to investigate the crystal structure of the sonication assisted hydrothermal treated CQD sample (Fig. 3b). The pattern exhibited a single wide diffraction pattern around 23.97° corresponds to the JCPDS # 65-6212 of graphite, with a single sharp peak at 22° corresponds to silica (Cristobalite-JCPDS # 89-3606) suggests the formation of some silica crystallization with graphitic structure (Mondal et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2016). In addition to that, functional groups of the CQD colloids were analyzed using FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3c). The sharp peak at 3325 cm−1, 2903 cm−1, and 2360 cm−1 was assigned to the N-H/O-H stretching vibration, due to the carbon backbone materials like cellulose, hemicellulose, and atmospheric CO2, respectively. A sharp and small band at around 1645 cm−1 and 1443 cm−1 corresponds to the N-H and CH2 bending (Wang et al., 2017). Then, the peak at 1140 cm− 1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the Si-O-C group. Similarly, the band at 1030 cm−1 and 816 cm−1, 665 cm−1 arises due to the presence of Si-O-Si, Si-C, and Si-H stretching vibration confirms the existence of notable silica bonding with graphene structure. The prepared CQD possess multiple functional groups (e.g. -COOH, -CO, -NH2) at their surface along with oxygen and nitrogen-rich species. The zeta potential of CQD (Fig. 3d), is showing the surface charge potential of − 32.27 mV indicates good stability in double distilled water, this may be due to the presence of a carboxyl group at their surface (Radhakrishnan and Panneerselvam, 2018; Qian et al., 2018). This highly negatively surface charge may be due to the passivation of the functional group(s) to explore the possibility to use this CQD as sensing probes to chelate the organic molecules and metal ions in real and simulated samples.

The optical absorption and fluorescence spectra of biomass corn cob derived carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been recorded using Fluorescence Spectrophotometer ( Fig. 4). Fig. 4a depicts the UV–Vis absorption spectra of sonication assisted hydrothermal synthesis of CQD. A broad absorption band is observed from 250 nm to 550 nm. The absorption band at 265 nm followed by the smaller shoulder at 350 nm is attributed to π-π* transitions of the CC bond and n-π* transitions of the CO bond, respectively (Joseph and Anappara, 2016). The bandgap is calculated from the UV spectra using the Tauc plot method and found to be around 2.92 eV. Below 250 nm an absorbance peak is observed at 235 nm which is related to silica (Inset: Fig. 4a) (Zheng et al., 2017). The emission spectrum has been recorded for CQD with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and the excitation spectra for the fixed emission of 450 nm is shown in Fig. S2. This result gives valid proof for the fluorescence property of the synthesized CQD. The quantum yield of the synthesized CQD is calculated to be 53.46% with quinine sulfate as standard (Fig. S5). Also, compared to the as-prepared samples, the hydrothermal treatment of CQD shows a 90% increase in emission intensity (Fig. S3). Fig. 4b represents the 2D spectral excitation vs. emission matrix of CQD. The spectral emission maximum is spread from the region of 370–550 nm with a narrow excitation of 300–370 nm. This broad emission gives us the possibility for blue light enhanced white emission of the CQD for λex = 350 nm, with the visible range emission (Inset image of Fig. 4b). This suggests that the prepared CQD by the hydrothermal method can contain the carbon core with defects that leads to significant strong white emission (Carvalho et al., 2019). The corresponding 3D spectral excitation vs. emission matrix surface plot (Fig. 4c) obtained from the combination of PL spectra of CQD at various excitation, further evidence the broad visible range emission of CQD with variable intensities. This high definition contour plot from the 3D plot shows two overlap regions from maximum emission from 350 nm to 420 nm (blue to green region) and further tailing with another second-order maximum form 420–675 nm i.e. towards hyperchromic (red) shift (in both 2D (Fig. S4) and 3D plots (Fig. 4c)).

The photostability of CQD at various pH conditions is examined from its emission spectra at λex-350 nm is shown in Fig. 4d. The stability and reversibility of the pH sensing of CQD have been duplicated with the rapid changing of pH (1–12) using HCL (for acidic variation) and NaOH (for base variation) (Mondal et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2017). The emission intensity of CQD is very stable from pH 12 up to pH 4 and decreases at lower pH (i.e. pH 1) condition (Fig. S6). This has been well expressed with associated fluorescence intensity that exhibits higher emission when the pH value reaches 8. Further, this may be because of the large quantity of carboxyl group on the surface of CQD confirmed from pure CQD FTIR spectra, which can interact with the H+ ions. The surface of CQD may be protonated at lower pH levels which results in a fluorescence quenching effect at this condition. Moreover, in order to understand the photostability at prolonged shelf life, the photoluminescence spectrum of CQD has been studied with an interval period of up to three months. Fig. 4e depicts the PL emission spectrum of CQD at various storage periods, in which we observed that there is no shift in the maximum intensity during this prescribed period. This confirms the potential ability of the prepared CQD stored at room temperature under neutral pH conditions with outstanding stability. This can be achieved by means of electron transitions between π-π* and n-π*, which have the potential to restore or deplete their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) band upon changes in the pH. A smaller increase in emission intensity from its first day might be due to the aggregation of CQD due to the influence of silica particles (Chen et al., 2014). Fig. 4f represents with reference to the 1931 Chromaticity (CIE) diagram, we have calculated the parameters from the PL spectrum of CQD at an excitation of 350 nm. The exceptional broad emission coordinates for overall spectra are found to be X = 0.18 and Y= 0.21 (the region in the blue spectrum)(Hindenberg et al., 2019) and the gaussian fitted PL spectra has the coordinates of X = 0.39 and Y= 0.36 (the region in the orange spectrum) (Narwal et al., 2019). It can also observe from the PL emission spectra, the broad region is covering the regions of yellow band region. From this, it can be seen that the CQD has a broad emission from blue to red region, that results in the overlap of these colors with blue enhanced white emission. These types of multipoint fitting were already reported for validating multiple emission from the compound with a single excitation (Ghosh and Prasad, 2015; Bhunia et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on TCSPC, deconvoluted PL spectrum, and band structure analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Time correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) is an effective method to determine fluorescence lifetimes of the specimens from picosecond to the microsecond. In the view of understanding the white emission of CQD, the energy alignment and bandgap also influence the fluorescence nature of the carbon dot (Sciortino et al., 2017). However, the emission mechanism of carbon dots is still debatable and a unique model is yet to explain (Lu et al., 2017). For our studies, fluorescence lifetime was measured using the time-correlated single-photon counting measurements (TCSPC) is shown in Fig. 5a. The emission spectrum 450 nm is observed with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The decay curve fitted as triexponential fitting with the χ 2 = 1.08 and therefore it has opens the possibility of three-lifetime components with slow, intermediate, and fast decay (Table S1). The multi-lifetime presence in the single-component reveals the presence of multiple emissive groups (fluorophores) present in the carbon quantum dots (Joseph and Anappara, 2016; Mintz et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2016). From the decay curve, the calculated average lifetime is 5.72 ns (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2016). This shorter lifetime justified the radiative recombination of electron and hole pair give rise to the fluorescence property of CQD and hence applicable in biological and optoelectronic fields (Gao et al., 2019; Yuan et al., 2019). In order to understand the mechanism of fluorescence through the recombination of channels, the peaks were deconvoluted using Gaussian peaks is shown in Fig. 5b (Yang et al., 2017). The broad blue emission peak at (2.35 eV) corresponds to the non-bridging oxygen hole (O3=Si-O.) and carbon defect centers (Joseph and Anappara, 2016). The green emission peak at 2.57 eV may be attributed to Si-C and the blue emission peak at 2.85 eV reveals that, oxygen vacancy presence in the porous structure of carbon dots (Fabbri et al., 2012). The peak at 3.1 eV is due to the scattering of incident excitation light. The excitation dependent luminescence origin can be ascribed by the electronic transitions from the HOMO and the adjacent state of LUMO illustrated in Fig. 5c. The abundant surface groups of CQD may introduce various energy levels in the energy gap positions. Even though these multiple and different electronic transitions modes lead to different emissions, the total combination of emissions gives rise to white emission (Hu et al., 2015; Li et al., 2015). This white emission is possible due to the silica present in the carbon structure, the synthesized CQD with Si may pay a new origin of photoluminescence characteristics and optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the quenching effect of CQD as nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The important applications of this biomass-derived CQD as a nanoprobe is used for sensing various organic molecules: deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), urea, paracetamol drug (APAP), thiourea, and ammonium persulfate (APS); inorganic materials: potassium (K+), magnesium (Mg2+), zinc (Zn2+), strontium (Sr2+), barium (Ba2+), mercury (Hg2+), nickel (Ni2+), zirconium (Zr2+), lead (Pb2+), copper (Cu2+), bismuth (Bi3+), boron (B3+), aluminum (Al3+), lanthanum (La3+), neodymium (Nd3+), ferrous (Fe3+), and chromium (Cr3+). The PL quenching experiments has been exemplified with the gradual addition of each solution to the CQD solutions and the observed photoluminescence emission spectra under excitation of 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of organic molecules and inorganic ions
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the fluorescence properties of CQD is studied for their interesting application towards the detection of organic molecules. Among the numerous organic molecules (Figs. S7 and S8), the dsDNA and paracetamol (APAP) show linear sensing in the optimized concentration of 1 ng/mL to 10 ng/mL, and 0.10–0.30 mg/mL, respectively. The fluorescence quenching effect has shown a good linear variation with the calculated parameters of the correlation coefficient, limit of detection, relative standard deviation (RSD), and R2 is 1–10 ng/mL, 2.5446 ng/mL, 2.30%, and 0.9878. Similarly, the above-mentioned parameters are 0.10–0.30 mg/mL, 0.0694 mg/mL, 4.46%, and 0.9999, for paracetamol (APAP), respectively. Among various organic molecules (e.g. DNA, BSA, urea, APS, thiourea, and APAP drug), the fluorescence emission of CQD in presence of DNA and APAP has shown higher quenching. This is may be due to the physisorption between the organic molecules and CQD surface functional group moieties. This result shows the higher linear concentration and detection limit compared to that of earlier reports towards the detection of DNA and APAP (Loo et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2017).

The fluorescence quenching effect of CQD using various 2+ oxidative state metal ions is demonstrated (Figs. S9, S10, and S11). Among this, Zr2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ ions have been optimized and evaluated under linear concentration range from 0.3 to 1.5 μM/mL, 0.26–2.35 μM/mL, and 0.43–4.72 μM/mL, respectively. The Zr2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ ions value of limit of detection, relative standard deviation (RSD) and R2 is 1.2454 μM/mL, 0.8641 μM/mL, and 1.3010 μM/mL; 1.84%, 1.79%, and 0.26%; and their corresponding correlation coefficient of 0.9511, 0.9867, and 0.9883, respectively. In order to compare the optical biosensor activity of CQD in presence of various 2+ oxidative state materials mentioned above, notable sensing by means of significant quenching effect is observed for Zr2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+. This is may be due to the chelation of Zr2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ ions by the silica-oxygen trap site in CQD. These results shows the higher dynamic concentration range and limit of detection compared with the earlier work of carbon quantum dots towards the detection of Cu2+ and Pb2+ ions (Kumari et al., 2018; Han et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017d; Gu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2018).

An effective fluorescence quenching response of CQD is observed when detecting Fe3+ and Cr3+ metal ions (Fig. S12) with good linearity in the gradual addition at the concentration of 0.78–3.9 μM/mL and 0.05–2.5 μM/mL, respectively. The estimated limit of detection is 0.8550 μM/mL, and 2.8562 μM/mL for Fe3+ and Cr3+, respectively. Moreover, the calculated relative standard deviation and R2 are 1.23% and 0.996; 0.35% and 0.9185, respectively. The probable quenching mechanism is, when the incident light illuminated upon the CQD, the exciton recombination rapidly occurs resulting in fluorescence emission. However, CQD has low electronegativity due to the presence of various surface functional group moieties. Meanwhile, in the presence of Fe3+, the 5d orbital has been half-filled in the outer electron structure; so the excited electrons from CQD are inclined to transition to the Fe3+ of d orbital, thus form as nonradiative recombination resultant fluorescence quenching (Li et al., 2018). Meantime, the feasible quenching mechanism is quite distinct for Cr3+, when the light illuminated on the CQD can absorb the energy and the ground state electron transition to excited state; however, the excited electron of CQD transition to Cr3+ which form nonradiative recombination of electrons resultant would be fluorescence quenching (Feng et al., 2019). With this contrast, the obtained result shows higher in the linear range and detection limit which can be compared with an earlier report for the detection of Fe3+ ions (Qi et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020) and Cr3+ ions (Feng et al., 2019; Mondal et al., 2019), respectively. All these results can be comparable with the earlier reports, tabulated in Supplementary Table S3. As a result, the proposed CQD can act as an efficient fluorescence nanoprobe and are an excellent candidate for dsDNA, paracetamol drug, Zr2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ ion detection (Table S2).

The relationship between the quenching ratio and the concentration of samples confirms from the linear fit correlations and given in Fig. 5d. The observed result with the error bar shows the distinct variation in the fluorescence efficiency. It can be noted that the material possesses highly significant selectivity towards the detection of dsDNA, APAP, Zr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ metal ions in comparison to other materials. The selectivity of dsDNA, APAP, Zr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ metal ions are found to be 43%, 60%, 77%, 78% 82%, 97%, and 99%, respectively. From this CQD, can be suggested as a nanoprobe to detect the organic molecules, 2+ and 3+ oxidative metal ions in the aqueous solution. Altogether, the organic molecules, 2+ and 3+ oxidative metal ions possess more affinity and rapid chelating activity. This may be due to the presence of carboxylate and organo-nitrogen groups occurrence in the synthesized CQD (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015).

In order to measure the precision and stability of these specific discriminating materials, the fluorescence emission spectra of CQD was evaluated in the presence of the highest concentration of analytes for consecutive three days (Wang et al., 2016a). The ratio of the dsDNA, APAP, Zr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ metal ions have been fixed about 10 ng/mL, 0.30 mg/mL, 1.5515 μM/mL, 2.35 μM/mL, 4.7293 μM/mL, 3.94 μM/mL and 2.5 μM/mL with n = 3. The calculated results exhibited that the high intra-day precision with variation only of 2.30%, 4.46%, 1.84%, 1.79%, 0.26%, 1.23%, and 0.35% (RSD=3) for dsDNA, APAP, Zr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ metal ions, respectively. This result ensured the reproducibility and discriminating of materials and further confirms the significance of CQD as an effective optical nanoprobe with good stability.

Often, most CQD are sensitive to organic molecules and oxidative state metal ions this is due to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at its surface. Advantageously the biomass-derived CQD has an indigenous higher amount of carboxyl and hydroxyl group presence and hence shows greater efficacy towards optical sensors, through fluorescence quenching effect. Also, this quenching effect might be developed by the formation of CQD-oxidative state metal ions or organic molecules complexes. Another possibility of this fluorescence quenching of CQD may be typically through a photoinduced electron transfer process. When the electrons were excited to the higher energy level through photon incidence, the excited electrons thereby have recombination with the nearby organic molecules/oxidative state metal ions ( Fig. 6). This electron recombination results in fluorescence quenching with respect to the concentration of the sensing molecules. These results significantly evident that the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQD is an essential key factor for sensitive and selective detection of organic molecules and oxidative state metal ions (Zhang et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of flexible white emission CQD sheet
ce:section.ce:para: The emission of light from abundant organic, inorganic, and hybrid or composite based material is having an exceptional phenomenon due to its potential application in fabricating the light-emitting device. Organic/inorganic luminescence devices, fluorescence lamps, and organic light-emitting devices receive a great intention as solid-state lighting materials that are found to be an alternative to incandescent lamps. Besides, commercially available white light emission lamps are composed of rare-earth-doped phosphor material or addition of toxic fluorophores (Bose et al., 2018; Primputkar et al., 2009). To overcome this, the development of novel, low energy consumption, and reliable white light emission remains as an unsolved challenge (Tanwar et al., 2019). More reports evidence on sustainable synthesized CQD with green, blue, and red emission (Liu et al., 2020a, 2020b; Boruah et al., 2020). Sustainably synthesized CQD also has ease of synthesis, high QY, good brightness with stability, cheap starting materials, has greater water-solubility, and exceptional toxicity effect (Kumar et al., 2020). With this connection, it may be possible to scale up the production of sustainable synthesis of fluorescence CQD at a lesser cost, and without using any adverse chemicals. As from our work, we have developed white light-emitting CQD without adding any additional fluorophores, we extended the work for developing the CQD colloids to white light-emitting films.

The schematic representation of the process of fabricating white light-emitting sheets is given in Fig. 7. Briefly, 1 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was added into the 10 mL of the prepared colloidal carbon quantum dots solution and heated at 100 °C, results in a transparent solution (Gao et al., 2019). This solution (CQD nanofluid) was poured into the predesigned template made by vinyl chloride-acetate copolymer resin or polyester or acrylic resin (e.g. commercial overhead projector sheet) and kept in the hot air oven at 80 °C for 24 h. As a consequence, the dried carbon quantum dot sheet was peeled off using stainless tweezers, and the fabricated sheet shows the highly transparent and foldable, depicted in Fig. 7a. The transparency of the sheet is very high (Fig. 7a) and it can be seen that Anna University Logo can be seen clearly from the top of the sheet. The pictorial representation of the prepared sheet in the absence of light illumination (Fig. 7b), under Xenon lamp with the excitation of 350 nm (Fig. 7c), and under UV with the excitation of 395 nm (Fig. 7d) shows the white emission from the sheet. A unique solid-state white emission CQD sheet can be fabricated by the aforementioned method which could be widely applied towards of QLED (Zheng et al., 2017). Notably, organic dye and semiconductor QD embedded in substrates (e.g. film, glass, sapphire, etc.) for QLED can be developed only by using sophisticated methods of chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and photolithography technique. In order to overcome this, our method of CQD sheet could be taken into the account owing to low-cost fabrication, flexibility, transparency, high stability in the ambient condition, and proves its potential ability to become a next-generation material for application in LED (Liu et al., 2019). The photoluminescence spectrum of CQD (Fig. S13a) after formation it as film also evidences its higher stability. The stability of CQD film were maintained in ambient conditions even after around 17 months without a change in their white emission (Fig. S13b and c).

In conclusion, we have achieved white emission fluorescence CQD from biomass by hydrothermal synthesis method which is facile, low-cost, highly stable, and can work under ambient conditions. The photoluminescence intensity of the CQD shows good sensing property towards dsDNA, APAP, Zr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ metal ions. This suggests the use of CQD colloids as an excellent candidate for the detection of these organic molecules and heavy metal ions with the significant limitations of detection, sensitivity, selectivity, precision, and stability. In addition to this, it might be also considered an effective tool for rapid discrimination of environmental toxic materials, pharmacological sensors, and other various fields in the forthcoming eras. The CQD colloids can also open the possibility to fabricate into luminescent sheets, without losing its white emission property, proves the scalability, foldability, and user-friendly possibilities. The stability of CQD film were examined in ambient conditions even after around 17 months without a change in their white emission. The fabricated CQD sheet is highly suitable for the next-generation carbon-based quantum dots with white emission QLED device.
